Cephalexin.correct: Cephalexin covers E. coli UTI effectively, has no interaction with valproate, and is safe in G6PD deficiency. It is the clean first-line choice when all other options carry specific contraindications in this patient.
Cephalexin.mechanism: First-generation cephalosporin. Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins. Covers gram-positive cocci and some gram-negative organisms including E. coli.
Cephalexin.clinical_use: Uncomplicated UTIs, skin and soft tissue infections, streptococcal pharyngitis. Oral bioavailability makes it ideal for outpatient use.
Cephalexin.key_facts: Safe in penicillin allergy (cross-reactivity <2%). No significant CYP interactions. Dose-adjust in renal impairment. Safe in G6PD deficiency.

wrong.Ciprofloxacin: Fluoroquinolones lower the seizure threshold and can reduce valproate serum levels through CYP interactions — a dangerous combination in a patient with epilepsy already on anticonvulsant therapy.
wrong.Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole: Sulfonamides cause oxidative stress on red blood cells. In G6PD deficiency, the cell cannot neutralize this stress, triggering hemolytic anemia. Hard contraindication.
wrong.Nitrofurantoin: Also causes oxidative hemolysis in G6PD-deficient patients through the same mechanism as sulfonamides. Both TMP-SMX and Nitrofurantoin are traps for the same reason — G6PD deficiency catches them both.

Levofloxacin.correct: Levofloxacin covers both typical and atypical CAP organisms, is safe in penicillin allergy, and requires only dose adjustment for CKD stage 3 — not avoidance. It has no significant interaction with warfarin at standard doses.
Levofloxacin.mechanism: Respiratory fluoroquinolone. Inhibits DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, blocking bacterial DNA replication. Broad spectrum covering gram-positives, gram-negatives, and atypicals.
Levofloxacin.clinical_use: Community-acquired pneumonia, complicated UTIs, skin infections. 'Respiratory fluoroquinolone' because of superior S. pneumoniae coverage vs ciprofloxacin.
Levofloxacin.key_facts: Dose-reduce in CKD (eGFR-based dosing). Safe with penicillin allergy. Monitor QTc. Weak CYP1A2 inhibitor — minimal warfarin interaction at standard doses.

wrong.Amoxicillin-clavulanate: This is a penicillin-class antibiotic. The patient had anaphylaxis to penicillin — an absolute contraindication. Cross-reactivity risk is real and this must be eliminated immediately.
wrong.Azithromycin: Azithromycin monotherapy is insufficient for CAP in an elderly patient with significant comorbidities. It also carries QT prolongation risk in a patient with AFib. Not the right choice here.
wrong.Clarithromycin: Clarithromycin is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor. It would dramatically increase warfarin's anticoagulant effect in this patient, creating serious bleeding risk. This interaction is a clinical contraindication.

Amoxicillin.correct: Amoxicillin is the first-line outpatient treatment for CAP caused by S. pneumoniae (Gram-positive diplococci) in a young healthy patient with no allergies or comorbidities. No reason to escalate therapy.
Amoxicillin.mechanism: Aminopenicillin. Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis. Extended spectrum vs plain penicillin — covers gram-positive organisms including S. pneumoniae and some gram-negatives.
Amoxicillin.clinical_use: Outpatient CAP, otitis media, strep pharyngitis, H. pylori (triple therapy), sinusitis. First-line for S. pneumoniae.
Amoxicillin.key_facts: Oral bioavailability ~90%. Safe in pregnancy. No dose adjustment needed for normal renal function. Watch for maculopapular rash if EBV mononucleosis is missed.

wrong.Vancomycin: Vancomycin is reserved for MRSA, severe hospital-acquired infections, or patients who have failed other therapies. Using it for outpatient CAP in a healthy young adult is gross over-treatment and promotes resistance.
wrong.Meropenem: A broad-spectrum carbapenem is entirely inappropriate for a simple outpatient CAP. It is reserved for multi-drug resistant organisms and severe hospital infections. Massive antibiotic stewardship violation.
wrong.Doxycycline: Doxycycline is an alternative for atypical CAP coverage, but it is not the first-line agent for S. pneumoniae pneumonia. Amoxicillin has superior activity against this organism in a patient with no contraindications.
